skill.name: docs-sources
skill.description: Guides agents through selecting documentation fetch strategies, detecting documentation frameworks, and configuring registry entries for docs sources.
skill.summary: Choose reliable fetch strategies for documentation sources and registry configuration.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and weak-cryptography patterns. Review shows the weak-cryptography and Ruby-backtick labels are false positives from Markdown and ordinary words, while the command and network guidance is real but aligned with the skill purpose. Publish with a warning that this skill can cause agents to fetch arbitrary documentation URLs and run local command examples.

medium_findings.title: External Fetch Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill provides curl, jq, grep, python3, and browser automation commands for checking and fetching documentation. This is expected for the skill, but it can make agents contact arbitrary user-provided documentation hosts and process untrusted responses.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and semantically tied to the skill purpose. I found no hidden exfiltration target, but user-controlled URLs and network fetches create real operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Browser Automation Against Untrusted Sites
medium_findings.description: The browser strategy recommends launching Chrome debugging and using a Playwright wrapper to navigate documentation pages. This may execute untrusted JavaScript from target sites and should be used only in an isolated browser context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The browser automation workflow is clearly present and can load arbitrary documentation URLs. The risk is moderate because the behavior is user-visible and part of the intended fallback path.

medium_findings.title: Pipeline Executes Curl For Sitemap-Derived URLs
medium_findings.description: The sitemap strategy pipes URLs from a sitemap index into xargs and curl. This is useful for discovery, but it can amplify requests to URLs controlled by a remote sitemap.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The xargs curl pipeline is explicit and uses remote input from sitemap contents. It does not show shell injection, but it can trigger unreviewed outbound requests.

low_findings.title: Ruby Backtick Execution Labels Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer labeled many Markdown code fences and inline code spans as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These files are documentation, not Ruby source, so the label is inaccurate; command risks are evaluated separately.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is Markdown tables, code fences, or registry examples. I found no Ruby execution context in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Labels Are Word-Match False Positives
low_findings.description: High-severity weak-cryptography alerts match ordinary documentation words such as description, API descriptions, paths, and framework headings. No cryptographic operation, hash verification, or encryption routine is present in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The alleged weak-crypto locations are metadata, OpenAPI field descriptions, and JSON examples. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are examples for known documentation platforms, registry templates, or placeholder docs sites. They do not receive secrets or local data in the reviewed content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in documentation mappings and examples. I found no credential collection, encoded payloads, or covert destination.

low_findings.title: Standard Device File Access Is Benign Error Redirection
low_findings.description: The filesystem alert points to redirecting xmllint errors to /dev/null. This is a conventional way to suppress parser errors and does not read sensitive files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line redirects stderr only and does not expose or modify local files. A separate low-risk filesystem example writes fetched docs to a user-named output file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Shell Commands In Skill Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: Multiple instructions use curl and command pipelines to inspect remote documentation sources. Agents should confirm target domains and avoid sending credentials or internal URLs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are direct and intentional. The risk depends on the target URL selected by the user or agent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Crawl Fallback
dangerous_patterns.description: The fallback browser workflow can execute target-site JavaScript while collecting docs. Use an isolated browser profile and prefer raw or llms.txt sources first.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly recommends browser navigation when curl fails. This is legitimate but has a larger attack surface than static fetches.

content.value_statement: Documentation sites use many formats, which makes source setup slow and inconsistent. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through framework detection, fetch strategy choice, and registry configuration.
actual_capabilities: Selects between llms.txt, GitHub raw, OpenAPI, sitemap, browser crawl, and web crawl strategies.
Identifies common documentation frameworks from config files and project structure.
Provides command examples for checking docs endpoints, sitemaps, API specs, and rendered pages.
Supplies registry configuration examples for multiple documentation source types.
Maps common libraries to known documentation URLs and suggested strategies.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a complete automated crawler.
Network and browser commands should be reviewed before execution against private or internal URLs.
Framework detection depends on public config files and may miss custom documentation setups.
Browser automation requires local tooling and can be slower than raw content fetching.

use_cases.title: Add a New Library Docs Source
use_cases.description: Choose the best fetch method for a library documentation site and create a registry entry.

use_cases.title: Audit Documentation Coverage
use_cases.description: Compare project dependencies with known documentation sources and identify missing entries.

use_cases.title: Configure API Documentation Fetching
use_cases.description: Detect OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, or GraphQL sources and pair them with conceptual documentation.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Fetch Strategy
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one docs site
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the docs-sources skill to evaluate this documentation URL: [URL]. Tell me which fetch strategy to use and why.

prompt_templates.title: Detect the Documentation Framework
prompt_templates.scenario: Identify framework signals before adding a source
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this docs site and linked repository: [URL]. Check for llms.txt, GitHub raw docs, OpenAPI specs, sitemap files, and framework config files.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Registry Entry
prompt_templates.scenario: Prepare source configuration after strategy selection
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the docs-sources registry examples to draft a registry entry for [PROJECT]. Include the strategy, homepage, important paths, and any framework-specific fields.

prompt_templates.title: Review Documentation Source Risks
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before running fetch commands
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the proposed docs-source fetch plan for [URL]. Flag any risky commands, private URLs, browser automation needs, or rate-limit concerns before execution.

output_examples.input: Evaluate https://example.com/docs for a docs registry entry.
output_examples.output: Recommended strategy: check llms.txt first, then sitemap. The site appears to expose static documentation pages, so browser automation is not the first choice.

output_examples.input: Find the best source type for a GitHub-hosted Docusaurus project.
output_examples.output: Use a GitHub raw strategy when the repository exposes Docusaurus config and docs files. Capture the docs path, branch, and sidebar configuration.

output_examples.input: Review an API documentation source plan.
output_examples.output: Use the OpenAPI strategy for machine-readable endpoints and pair it with a conceptual docs source if guides or tutorials are separate.

best_practices: Check llms.txt and raw repository content before using browser automation.
Confirm target domains and avoid private network URLs before running fetch commands.
Record the framework, strategy, homepage, and important paths in the registry entry.
anti_patterns: Starting with blind web crawling before checking structured or raw sources.
Running browser automation against untrusted sites without an isolated profile.
Adding a registry entry without validating that the selected fetch strategy works.

faq.question: Does this skill fetch documentation by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides strategy guidance, command examples, and registry patterns for an agent or user to apply.

faq.question: Which fetch strategy should I try first?
faq.answer: Start with llms.txt when available, then GitHub raw content, then API specs, sitemap files, and browser automation.

faq.question: Can it handle JavaScript-rendered documentation?
faq.answer: Yes, it includes a browser automation fallback for sites where curl returns incomplete content.

faq.question: Does it support API documentation?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, and GraphQL schema discovery patterns.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The skill includes commands that can contact external or user-provided URLs, so targets should be reviewed before execution.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning for network fetching, shell command examples, and browser automation guidance.
